nodes.name: AFI Greatest Films
nodes.description: American Film Institute's 100 greatest American films

items.prompt: Which 1941 Orson Welles film about a newspaper magnate, featuring the dying word "Rosebud," has topped the AFI 100 Greatest Films list since its inception?
items.explanation:
#1 on both the 1998 and 2007 AFI lists
Orson Welles co-wrote (with Herman J. Mankiewicz), directed, produced, and starred in the film at age 25 — his feature debut
Widely believed to be based on William Randolph Hearst, who tried to suppress the film
Won only one Oscar: Best Original Screenplay (Welles and Herman J. Mankiewicz); lost Best Picture to *How Green Was My Valley*
Innovations include deep-focus photography, nonlinear narrative, low-angle shots showing ceilings, and long takes
"Rosebud" — the sled from Kane's childhood — is one of cinema's most famous plot devices
items.cardFront: #1 on AFI list — 1941, Orson Welles

items.prompt: Which 1972 Francis Ford Coppola crime epic about the Corleone family, starring Marlon Brando, ranks #2 on the AFI 100 Greatest Films list?
items.explanation:
#2 on the 2007 AFI list (also #3 on the 1998 list)
Brando refused his Best Actor Oscar; sent Sacheen Littlefeather to decline it in protest of Hollywood's treatment of Native Americans
Paramount wanted a cheap, contemporary film; Coppola fought for the period setting and three-hour runtime
"I'm gonna make him an offer he can't refuse" — AFI's #2 greatest movie quote
*The Godfather Part II* (1974) also won Best Picture — the only sequel to do so until *The Return of the King* (2003)
items.cardFront: #2 on AFI list — 1972, Coppola

items.prompt: Which 1942 wartime romance set in French Morocco, starring Humphrey Bogart and Ingrid Bergman, ranks #3 on the AFI 100 Greatest Films list?
items.explanation:
#3 on the 2007 AFI list (was #2 on the 1998 list)
Directed by Michael Curtiz; the script was still being written during filming — actors often did not know the ending
Famous quotes include "Here's looking at you, kid," "Of all the gin joints...," and "Play it, Sam" (often misquoted as "Play it again, Sam")
Won Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Screenplay at the 16th Academy Awards
Released weeks after the Allied invasion of North Africa, boosting its topicality and box office
items.cardFront: #3 on AFI list — 1942, Bogart and Bergman

items.prompt: Which 1980 Martin Scorsese boxing biopic, shot in black and white, stars Robert De Niro as middleweight champion Jake LaMotta and ranks #4 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#4 on the 2007 AFI list (was #24 on the 1998 list — a dramatic jump)
De Niro gained 60 pounds to play the older, retired LaMotta — one of the most famous physical transformations in film history
Won two Oscars: Best Actor (De Niro) and Best Film Editing (Thelma Schoonmaker)
Scorsese was initially reluctant to make a boxing film; De Niro spent years persuading him
The boxing sequences mix varying film speeds, different angles, and sometimes sound effects instead of music to visceral effect
LaMotta himself served as a technical consultant on the film
items.cardFront: #4 on AFI list — 1980, Scorsese/De Niro

items.prompt: Which 1952 musical comedy about Hollywood's transition from silent films to talkies, featuring a famous lamppost dance in a downpour, ranks #5 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#5 on the 2007 AFI list (was #10 on the 1998 list)
Co-directed by Gene Kelly and Stanley Donen; Kelly also starred and choreographed
Kelly performed the title number with a 103°F fever; the "rain" was made visible on camera through careful backlighting (a common myth claims milk was added to the water, but this has been debunked)
The film was built around a pre-existing catalog of Arthur Freed songs from the 1920s–1930s — a "jukebox musical" decades before the term existed
Debbie Reynolds was 19 with no dance training; Kelly's demands were so intense that Fred Astaire found her crying under a piano and let her watch one of his rehearsals to show that even professionals struggle
items.cardFront: #5 on AFI list — 1952, Gene Kelly

items.prompt: Which 1939 Civil War epic about Scarlett O'Hara, starring Vivien Leigh and Clark Gable, ranks #6 on the AFI list and remains the highest-grossing film ever adjusted for inflation?
items.explanation:
#6 on the 2007 AFI list (was #4 on the 1998 list)
Won eight competitive Oscars from 13 nominations; Hattie McDaniel became the first African American Oscar winner
Directed by Victor Fleming; produced by David O. Selznick
Adjusted for inflation, remains the highest-grossing film of all time at approximately $3.7 billion in today's dollars
The burning of Atlanta scene used old sets from *King Kong* and *The Garden of Allah*
"Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn" is AFI's #1 greatest movie quote
items.cardFront: #6 on AFI list — 1939, Civil War epic

items.prompt: Which 1962 David Lean epic about a British officer's role in the Arab Revolt during World War I, starring Peter O'Toole, ranks #7 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#7 on the 2007 AFI list (was #5 on the 1998 list)
Directed by David Lean; Peter O'Toole's breakthrough role as T.E. Lawrence
Won seven Oscars including Best Picture and Best Director; O'Toole lost Best Actor to Gregory Peck (*To Kill a Mockingbird*)
Shot on location in Jordan, Morocco, and Spain; the vast desert cinematography by Freddie Young is considered among the finest ever
Nearly four hours long; Steven Spielberg has called it the film that inspired him to become a director
O'Toole was nominated for eight Best Actor Oscars over his career but never won competitively
items.cardFront: #7 on AFI list — 1962, David Lean, Peter O'Toole

items.prompt: Which 1993 Steven Spielberg film, shot mostly in black and white, about a German industrialist who saved over 1,100 Jews during the Holocaust ranks #8 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#8 on the 2007 AFI list (was #9 on the 1998 list)
Won seven Oscars including Best Picture and Spielberg's first Best Director
Stars Liam Neeson as Oskar Schindler, Ralph Fiennes as SS officer Amon Göth, and Ben Kingsley as Itzhak Stern
The "girl in the red coat" is one of cinema's most memorable uses of selective color in an otherwise black-and-white film
Spielberg refused a salary, calling it "blood money," and used profits to establish the Shoah Foundation
John Williams composed the score; Itzhak Perlman performed the main violin theme
items.cardFront: #8 on AFI list — 1993, Spielberg, Holocaust

items.prompt: Which 1958 Alfred Hitchcock psychological thriller about a retired detective with a fear of heights who becomes obsessed with a mysterious woman ranks #9 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#9 on the 2007 AFI list (rose from #61 on the 1998 list — one of the biggest jumps in AFI history)
Stars James Stewart and Kim Novak; the "vertigo effect" (dolly zoom) was pioneered here and has been copied in hundreds of films since
In 2012, *Sight & Sound* magazine's critics' poll named it the greatest film ever made, dethroning *Citizen Kane* for the first time in 50 years
Received mixed reviews and modest box office on release; its reputation grew dramatically over decades
Hitchcock considered it his most personal film; themes of obsessive control mirror his own relationships with actresses
Bernard Herrmann's hypnotic score is considered one of the greatest in film history
items.cardFront: #9 on AFI list — 1958, Hitchcock, fear of heights

items.prompt: Which 1939 Victor Fleming musical fantasy, featuring a Kansas girl transported to a Technicolor world of witches, a yellow brick road, and ruby slippers, ranks #10 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#10 on the 2007 AFI list (was #6 on the 1998 list)
The transition from sepia-tone Kansas to Technicolor Oz is one of cinema's most iconic visual moments
Judy Garland was 16 during filming; "Over the Rainbow" almost got cut by the studio but became her signature song and won Best Original Song
The ruby slippers were silver in L. Frank Baum's 1900 novel; changed to red to showcase Technicolor
Budget was $2.8 million and initially lost money at the box office; it became profitable through re-releases and television broadcasts
"There's no place like home" is AFI's #23 greatest movie quote
items.cardFront: #10 on AFI list — 1939, Judy Garland, Oz

items.prompt: Which 1960 Alfred Hitchcock thriller about a secretary who steals money and checks into a remote motel run by a disturbed young man ranks #14 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#14 on the 2007 AFI list (was #18 on the 1998 list)
The shower scene used 78 camera setups and 52 cuts in 45 seconds; the "blood" was chocolate syrup (the film is in black and white)
Hitchcock demanded theaters not admit latecomers — a revolutionary policy that changed cinema exhibition forever
Anthony Perkins as Norman Bates set the template for the modern serial-killer character
Hitchcock financed the film himself after Paramount balked; it became his biggest commercial hit
Based on Robert Bloch's 1959 novel, itself inspired by Ed Gein, a Wisconsin serial killer
items.cardFront: #14 on AFI list — 1960, Hitchcock, Bates Motel

items.prompt: Which 1968 Stanley Kubrick science fiction film featuring the sentient computer HAL 9000, a mysterious black monolith, and a psychedelic "Star Gate" sequence ranks #15 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#15 on the 2007 AFI list (was #22 on the 1998 list)
Kubrick co-wrote the screenplay with Arthur C. Clarke; the novel and film were developed simultaneously
HAL 9000 is cinema's most iconic artificial intelligence; his calm voice saying "I'm sorry, Dave. I'm afraid I can't do that" is deeply unsettling
The film contains only about 40 minutes of dialogue in its 139-minute runtime
Kubrick destroyed most of the models after filming to prevent knock-offs and unauthorized use
Richard Strauss's *Also sprach Zarathustra* and Johann Strauss II's *The Blue Danube* became forever associated with the film
items.cardFront: #15 on AFI list — 1968, Kubrick, HAL 9000

items.prompt: Which 1950 Billy Wilder film noir about a faded silent-film star who ensnares a struggling screenwriter in her delusional world ranks #16 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#16 on the 2007 AFI list (was #12 on the 1998 list)
Gloria Swanson plays Norma Desmond, a reclusive former silent-film star; "I am big. It's the pictures that got small" is AFI's #24 greatest movie quote
William Holden narrates as the dead screenwriter floating face-down in a pool — the film opens with his corpse
Erich von Stroheim plays Max, Norma's butler and former director/husband — mirroring his real career as a director from the silent era
Louis B. Mayer reportedly confronted Wilder after a screening, shouting, "You have disgraced the industry that made and fed you!"
Nominated for 11 Oscars but won only three: Best Screenplay, Best Art Direction, Best Score
items.cardFront: #16 on AFI list — 1950, Wilder, Norma Desmond

items.prompt: Which 1967 Mike Nichols comedy-drama about a directionless young man seduced by an older woman, featuring Simon & Garfunkel's music, ranks #17 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#17 on the 2007 AFI list (was #7 on the 1998 list)
Dustin Hoffman's breakthrough role as Benjamin Braddock; Anne Bancroft plays Mrs. Robinson, who seduces him
"Mrs. Robinson, you're trying to seduce me" is AFI's #63 greatest movie quote
Simon & Garfunkel's "The Sound of Silence" and "Mrs. Robinson" became inseparable from the film
Nichols won Best Director; the film was nominated for seven Oscars but lost Best Picture to *In the Heat of the Night*
The ambiguous final scene — Benjamin and Elaine's smiles fading on the back of a bus — is one of cinema's most analyzed endings
items.cardFront: #17 on AFI list — 1967, Nichols, Mrs. Robinson

items.prompt: Which 1954 Elia Kazan film about a dockworker fighting union corruption, starring Marlon Brando and featuring the line "I coulda been a contender," ranks #19 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#19 on the 2007 AFI list (was #8 on the 1998 list)
Won eight Oscars from 12 nominations: Best Picture, Best Director, Best Actor (Brando), Best Supporting Actress (Eva Marie Saint)
Brando's taxi scene with Rod Steiger — "I coulda been a contender" — is one of the most iconic performances in cinema
Widely read as Kazan's defense of his testimony before the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1952
Shot on location in Hoboken, New Jersey, in the dead of winter; the cold is visible in the actors' breath
Eva Marie Saint's film debut; she later starred in Hitchcock's *North by Northwest* (1959)
items.cardFront: #19 on AFI list — 1954, Kazan, Brando, dockworkers

items.prompt: Which 1946 Frank Capra film about a suicidal small-town banker shown what life would be like without him by a guardian angel ranks #20 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#20 on the 2007 AFI list (was #11 on the 1998 list)
Stars James Stewart as George Bailey and Henry Travers as angel Clarence Odbody
Flopped at the box office on release and was considered a failure; became beloved decades later through repeated television broadcasts
Its copyright lapsed in 1974, allowing TV stations to air it freely, which transformed it into a Christmas tradition
Capra called it his favorite film; Stewart said it was the best script he ever read
Five Oscar nominations but no wins; lost Best Picture to *The Best Years of Our Lives*
items.cardFront: #20 on AFI list — 1946, Capra, guardian angel

items.prompt: Which 1974 Roman Polanski neo-noir about a private detective uncovering corruption and a dark family secret in 1930s Los Angeles ranks #21 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#21 on the 2007 AFI list (was #19 on the 1998 list)
Stars Jack Nicholson as J.J. Gittes and Faye Dunaway as Evelyn Mulwray; written by Robert Towne
Towne's screenplay is widely considered the greatest original screenplay ever written; won the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay
"Forget it, Jake. It's Chinatown" is the famous closing line, spoken not by the protagonist but by a colleague
Inspired by real Los Angeles water wars of the early 1900s, when William Mulholland diverted water from the Owens Valley
Nominated for 11 Oscars but won only one (Towne's screenplay); lost Best Picture to *The Godfather Part II*
items.cardFront: #21 on AFI list — 1974, Polanski, L.A. water scandal

items.prompt: Which 1959 Billy Wilder comedy about two musicians who disguise themselves as women to escape gangsters, starring Marilyn Monroe, ranks #22 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#22 on the 2007 AFI list (was #14 on the 1998 list)
Stars Jack Lemmon and Tony Curtis as the disguised musicians; Marilyn Monroe as Sugar Kane, the band's ukulele-playing singer
Closing line — "Well, nobody's perfect" — is AFI's #48 greatest movie quote and one of cinema's greatest final lines
Monroe required 47 takes for the line "It's me, Sugar" and reportedly needed cue cards for simple dialogue
Consistently tops critics' lists of the greatest comedy ever made
The film violated the Production Code by featuring cross-dressing and homosexual implications; released without Code approval
items.cardFront: #22 on AFI list — 1959, Wilder, Monroe

items.prompt: Which 1982 Steven Spielberg film about a boy who befriends a stranded alien and helps him "phone home" ranks #24 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#24 on the 2007 AFI list (was #25 on the 1998 list)
Melissa Mathison wrote the screenplay; Spielberg drew on his childhood feelings of loneliness after his parents' divorce
The flying-bicycle-across-the-moon shot became the logo for Spielberg's Amblin Entertainment production company
Grossed $797 million worldwide, surpassing *Star Wars* as the highest-grossing film of all time (held the record until *Jurassic Park* in 1993)
"E.T. phone home" is AFI's #15 greatest movie quote
Reese's Pieces sales increased 65% after the film; M&M's parent company Mars had turned down the product-placement deal
items.cardFront: #24 on AFI list — 1982, Spielberg, "phone home"

items.prompt: Which 1962 Robert Mulligan film about a lawyer defending a Black man falsely accused of rape in Depression-era Alabama, starring Gregory Peck, ranks #25 on the AFI list?
items.explanation:
#25 on the 2007 AFI list (was #34 on the 1998 list)
Gregory Peck won Best Actor; AFI named Atticus Finch the #1 greatest hero in American film history
Based on Harper Lee's 1960 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel; adapted by screenwriter Horton Foote
Filmed in Hollywood but set in the fictional town of Maycomb, Alabama, based on Lee's hometown of Monroeville
Peck and Lee became lifelong friends; she gave him her father's pocket watch
Also features the film debut of Robert Duvall as Boo Radley, in a non-speaking role
items.cardFront: #25 on AFI list — 1962, Mulligan, Atticus Finch
